channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: The 85-Year-Old Widow Snagged by Trump’s Immigration Crackdown
item.description: In her first interview since being deported, Marie-Thérèse Ross-Mahé, the French widow of a former G.I., recounted her experience in ICE detention.
item.dc:creator: Catherine Porter
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times

item.title: 2 Killed in Boat Strike in Eastern Pacific Ocean, Pentagon Says
item.description: The attack — the sixth this month — raised the death toll to at least 182 in a campaign against people the Trump administration accuses of smuggling drugs at sea.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US Strikes on Alleged Drug Traffickers (2025- )
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gen. Francis L. Donovan, the head of the Southern Command, ordered the latest boat attack.

item.title: Rubio’s Absence From Iran Talks Highlights Stay-at-Home Role
item.description: President Trump has outsourced much of his diplomacy to others, while Mr. Rubio focuses on his second job as national security adviser.
item.dc:creator: Michael Crowley
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: As President Trump prepares to send a delegation to the latest round of U.S.-Iran talks in Pakistan, his secretary of state, Marco Rubio, will remain where he often does: at home.

item.title: One of Two Missing USF Doctoral Students Is Found Dead, Officials Say
item.description: The University of South Florida students were reported missing last week. A roommate of the student whose body was found was charged in connection with his disappearance.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ziegler
item.media:credit: Hillsborough County Sheriff's Office, via Associated Press
item.media:description: A search continued on Friday for a missing University of South Florida doctoral students after the body of another student who had been reported missing was found on a bridge over Old Tampa Bay, officials said.

item.title: Two Men Plead Guilty in Harvard Medical School Explosion
item.description: Logan David Patterson, 18, and Dominick Frank Cardoza, 21, pleaded guilty for the November incident in which they set off a commercial firework in a research lab.
item.dc:creator: Sonia A. Rao
item.media:credit: Spencer Jones/Universal Images Group, via Getty Images
item.media:description: The explosion occurred on the fourth floor of the Goldenson Building, part of Harvard Medical School.

item.title: Wildfires Destroy Dozens of Structures in Georgia, Governor Says
item.description: Fires fueled by drought have burned tens of thousands of acres in southern Georgia and northern Florida, where officials said one blaze was blamed for the death of a volunteer firefighter.
item.dc:creator: Mark Walker and Amy Graff
item.media:credit: Sean Rayford/Getty Images
item.media:description: The Highway 82 fire in rural Brantley County, Ga., has burned more than 7,500 acres and 87 homes and other structures, Gov. Brian Kemp said.

item.title: 31 Sloths Acquired by an Orlando Animal Attraction Died, Officials Say
item.description: Many of the deaths were attributed to a lack of heat at an unauthorized warehouse that Sloth World was using, officials said.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor
item.media:credit: Fox 35 Orlando
item.media:description: The sloths were going to be the latest attraction at Sloth World in Orlando, Fla.

item.title: The ‘Lasting Damage’ of Pirro’s Investigation of the Federal Reserve and Powell
item.description: The Trump administration’s attacks on the Federal Reserve have rattled confidence in the central bank’s ability to operate independently before a leadership transition.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith and Tony Romm
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump visited the Federal Reserve’s Washington headquarters in July during renovations.

item.title: Republicans’ Likely Strategy as Midterms Near: Negative Campaigning
item.description: Republicans are likely to fall back on a tried-and-tested strategy for the midterms: Going negative.
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times

item.title: Over 200,000 Heated Socks Sold at Costco Recalled After Reports of Burns
item.description: The socks, from the brand 32 Degrees, were linked to reports of first- and second-degree burns, according to U.S. product safety regulators.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:credit: Graham Dickie/The New York Times
item.media:description: The recalled socks were sold exclusively at Costco, where customers can return them and seek refunds, the company said in its recall notice.

item.title: Why Are Politicians Dropping So Many F-Bombs?
item.description: Times reporters had a hunch that politicians were using the F-word more than ever. So they analyzed the data and found an interesting trend: Democrats are swearing far more than Republicans. Our reporter Kellen Browning breaks down why.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning, Mimi Dwyer, Yasu Tsuji, June Kim and Karl Mollohan

item.title: Maine Governor Vetoes Bill That Would Have Paused New Data Centers
item.description: Gov. Janet Mills said she rejected what would have been the nation’s first moratorium on data centers because it failed to exempt a project in a distressed mill town.
item.dc:creator: Jenna Russell
item.media:credit: Jonathan Ernst/Reuters
item.media:description: An aerial view of an Amazon Web Services data center in Ashburn, Va.

item.title: Appeals Court Says Trump’s Ban on Asylum Claims at Border Is Illegal
item.description: The ruling could require the Trump administration to begin processing new applications from asylum seekers at the southern border.
item.dc:creator: Mattathias Schwartz
item.media:credit: Alejandro Cegarra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Migrants approved for asylum appointments with Customs and Border Protection last year in Tapachula, Mexico.

item.title: Camp Mystic May Not Receive License to Reopen After Fatal Flood in Texas
item.description: A review by state officials in Texas said the camp, hit by catastrophic flooding last year, was not in compliance with safety requirements.
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman and Ruth Graham
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: Some of Camp Mystic’s emergency plans were judged incomplete, insufficient or missing by the Texas Department of State Health Services.

item.title: An Unthinkable Extreme of Domestic Violence: Killing Multiple Relatives
item.description: The Shreveport, La., slaying of eight children was an example of what experts call family annihilation.
item.dc:creator: Christina Morales
item.media:credit: Brandon Bell/Getty Images
item.media:description: A memorial set up in Shreveport, La., after a father killed eight children, seven of whom were his, according to the police.

item.title: Trump Says He Dislikes Prediction Markets. His Family Invests in Them.
item.description: The White House has warned staff not to wager on government decisions, but his family’s involvement with these firms undermines the president’s message.
item.dc:creator: Ben Protess
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump’s eldest son, Donald Trump Jr., has ties to two of the industry’s top prediction market firms, including Polymarket, a platform that prosecutors say a U.S. soldier used to place bets using classified information.

item.title: Trump Administration Approves Firing Squad Executions for Death Penalty
item.description: The Justice Department also reauthorized the use of a death penalty drug, and will seek to shorten the length of some legal appeals.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett and Nicholas Bogel-Burroughs
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Justice Department has reauthorized the use of pentobarbital in executions of federal inmates and will also allow the use of firing squads and “additional methods of execution.”

item.title: White House Allowed Officials’ Text Messages to Be Deleted, Lawsuit Says
item.description: Two watchdogs say internal White House guidance that text messages need not be preserved unless “they are the sole record of official decision-making” contradicted the law.
item.dc:creator: Minho Kim
item.category: Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: The lawsuit comes amid a torrent of accusations that President Trump and his administration have disregarded record-keeping and document disclosure required by law.

item.title: Deb Haaland’s Historic Run for New Mexico Governor Hits a Primary Snag
item.description: In New Mexico, the former interior secretary seemed to have an easy path to becoming the first Native American woman elected governor — until Sam Bregman challenged her.
item.dc:creator: Reis Thebault
item.media:credit: Nina Riggio for The New York Times
item.media:description: Deb Haaland, former Interior secretary and congresswoman, seemed like a shoo-in for New Mexico governor, but she finds herself in a real Democratic primary race.

item.title: How Kevin Warsh Could Shrink the Fed’s Footprint in Financial Markets
item.description: President Trump’s nominee to become the next chair of the Federal Reserve wants to overhaul the central bank, including its more than $6 trillion balance sheet.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: “It took 18 years to create this balance sheet problem, and we won’t be able to fix it in 18 minutes,” Kevin M. Warsh, the Federal Reserve chair nominee, told lawmakers on Tuesday.

item.title: Tariffs Raised Consumers’ Prices, but the Refunds Go Only to Businesses
item.description: Many families felt the sting of the president’s now-illegal tariffs, but companies have said little about whether they will share the $166 billion coming back to them.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: The biggest beneficiaries of tariff refunds may include retail giants like Costco, even if companies passed the costs onto their customers.

item.title: A New Worry for Republicans: Latino Catholics Offended by Trump
item.description: In 2024, the president made significant inroads with Hispanics. But in a competitive Arizona congressional district, voters now express dismay at his feud with the pope.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Medina
item.media:credit: Adriana Zehbrauskas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Parishioners praying after service at the St. Augustine Cathedral on Monday in downtown Tucson, Ariz.

item.title: Todd Blanche Targets Trump’s Enemies Amid Jockeying to Lead DOJ
item.description: Mr. Blanche’s actions are meant to demonstrate progress on the president’s priorities, chief among them payback.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush, Alan Feuer and Kenneth P. Vogel
item.media:credit: Chip Somodevilla/Getty Images
item.media:description: Todd Blanche, the acting attorney general, has made it clear to senior White House officials that he plans to move more efficiently than his predecessor, Pam Bondi.

item.title: The A.A.U.P. Is Growing Fast as It Ramps Up Its Fight Against Trump
item.description: The American Association of University Professors is drawing new members. The group’s critics say its political stances hurt its cause.
item.dc:creator: Vimal Patel
item.media:credit: Emon Hassan for The New York Times
item.media:description: Todd Wolfson, the president of American Association of University Professors, has led the organization as it has become one of the Trump administration’s most aggressive adversaries.
